reason: Cannot be refuted on the asked axes: this is a Phase-3 deferral POLICY, not a shippable prompt/severity/fixture change. Verified prompt_rewrite=='(none)' and fixture_edits==[], so it (a) cannot under-flag any defect class, (b) cannot loosen a severity/threshold to let a known-bad fixture slip, and (c) touches no fixture, so it cannot make a bad case ambiguous or dirty a good case. Its substance is the CONSERVATIVE direction: it forbids widening any fp_max to absorb the Phase-0c baseline over-flags (compliance/data-integrity fp 1.00, env-parity 0.67, observability 0.83, security/test-quality/usability 0.33) on the grounds — confirmed by BASELINE-SUMMARY.md — that every elevated fp is a prompt/fixture defect, not real over-flagging, so widening would mask future regressions. It defers re-derivation until AFTER the coupled prompt+fixture fixes land, then re-derives every recall_min/fp_max via `framework eval-analyze` (verified to exist at src/framework_cli/cli.py:1078, derives recall_min=recall-margin). observability-db's threshold is set null/deferred, which is correct because its Phase-0c recall 0.00 is a degenerate-fixture artifact (good fixture byte-identical to bad; no true-positive signal) — pinning a numeric floor now would bake that broken baseline into the gate. Refuting (i.e. demanding thresholds be re-derived now against the known-defective baseline) is the actually-harmful option. Ship it.
